text: The surgeon begins the operation by sweeping the small intestine to the right of the abdomen and towards the pelvis. The cecum is elevated to reveal the tenting of the right colic artery. Dissection is performed around the artery in preparation for transection with a vascular stapler.

The surgeon uses the transected side to access and dissect the mesentery from the retroperitoneum, elevating the colon medially to laterally. The dissection progresses to the hepatic flexure, with the duodenum identified and protected. The transverse colon is dissected free from the omentum, proximal to the middle colic artery.

The surgeon rolls the transverse colon and hepatic flexure in a caudal direction to facilitate dissection. A tunnel is created under the transverse colon for stapled transection at the distal end of the specimen, requiring two staple loads for complete transection. The hepatic flexure is dissected free, and the right hemicolon is visibly mobilized.

The surgeon connects the dissection planes from the hepatic flexure to the medial to lateral dissection. The lateral attachments of the ascending colon are taken down, freeing it. The proximal small intestine is transected approximately two centimeters proximal to the ileocecal valve. The right hemicolon is elevated, revealing the remaining mesentery attachments, which are then transected with a vessel sealer, freeing the entire specimen.

The terminal ileum is positioned near the transverse colon in an isoparastaltic manner. Endocyanin green dye is injected, and the fluorescence lens of the da Vinci robot is used to visualize the vascular perfusion of the anastomotic limbs, showing bright fluorescence indicating adequate blood flow.

The surgeon places stay sutures at the proximal and distal ends of the planned anastomosis for intestinal manipulation. Enterotomies are created in both the colon and small intestine, followed by a side-by-side stapled anastomosis.

The surgeon inspects the anastomosis, confirming no intraluminal bleeding from the staple line. The common channel enterotomy is over-sewn in two layers: the full thickness mucosal layer is closed with a running 3-0 Vicryl suture, followed by imbrication of the suture line with a 2-0 silk suture to close the second serosal layer.
The surgeon briefly rechecks the vascular perfusion of the anastomotic limbs using fluorescence imaging. The sutures are removed, indicating the completion of the operation.
